food_name: Inari Udon with Sushi and Drink Bundle

english: Inari Udon with Sushi and Drink

food_subgroup: Japanese noodle and sushi meal set

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for inari udon specifically; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor. The bundle set meal aspect is not captured by any single FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for inari udon or Japanese udon set meals. A00LC (Wheat noodles) is used as the closest parent for the udon component. The sushi and drink components are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for inari udon or Japanese noodle set meals.

serving.description: 1 bundle set (udon bowl + 1 sushi piece + 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One bundle set consisting of approximately 300 g inari udon bowl (noodles, broth, inari tofu), one sushi piece (approximately 50 g), and one drink (approximately 200 ml), totalling an estimated 550 g edible weight excluding the beverage volume.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle soup with tofu (inari udon) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Japanese noodle dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'inari udon with sushi and drink bundle' as a composite meal. Nutrient values are estimated by component: udon noodle soup (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 35188 and related entries for Japanese udon soup), inari age seasoned tofu (USDA FoodData Central, NDB 16426), and sushi rice with nori (USDA SR Legacy). Values are blended per estimated component weights (udon bowl ~300 g, sushi ~50 g, drink excluded from per-100g solid calculation) and expressed per 100 g of the solid food portion. This is a medium-confidence estimate. The beverage component is excluded from per-100g nutrient calculation as it is variable (water, green tea, or soft drink).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for inari udon with sushi bundle as a composite meal. GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles (white wheat udon, GI approximately 55, low category), which constitutes the dominant glycemic component (~300 g of the ~350 g solid food portion). Inari age (seasoned tofu) has negligible glycemic contribution. Sushi rice has a higher GI (~72) but contributes only ~50 g to the meal. Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 55 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 55 × available carbohydrate per serving ~43.9 g) / 100 = approximately 24.1, rounded to 24.0. Serving carbohydrate estimated from 350 g solid food at 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 55.3 g total carbohydrate minus fibre 2.8 g = ~52.5 g available; adjusted for sushi rice higher carbohydrate density giving blended estimate of ~43.9 g available carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single inari udon bundle may contribute approximately 1,045 mg sodium per serving (380 mg per 100 g × 275 g solid food), representing over 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from refined wheat udon noodles and sushi rice, but moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the udon noodles and sushi rice in this bundle have a combined low GI of approximately 55, but a moderate glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving, which may still cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; pairing with non-starchy vegetables or choosing a smaller portion can help manage blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; low saturated fat relative to total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content of this meal set (estimated >1,000 mg per serving from broth and soy seasoning), which may adversely affect blood pressure; the saturated fat content is relatively low at 0.4 g per 100 g, and the inari tofu component provides plant-based protein, which is cardioprotective when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Inari Udon with Sushi and Drink Bundle is a Japanese meal set offered at Sushi Express restaurants in Singapore, consisting of a bowl of inari udon, one piece of sushi (customer's choice), and a drink. Inari udon is a traditional Japanese noodle dish featuring thick, chewy wheat udon noodles served in a clear, savoury dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, topped with inari age — sweet, seasoned deep-fried tofu pouches (aburaage) that are simmered in a mixture of soy sauce, mirin, and sugar until they absorb a rich, slightly sweet flavour. The broth is typically made from kombu and bonito flakes (katsuobushi), giving it a delicate umami depth. The udon noodles are soft yet springy in texture, and the inari tofu adds a contrasting sweetness and silkiness. The sushi component is one piece selected by the customer from the restaurant's menu, typically nigiri or maki, adding a small portion of vinegared sushi rice and a protein topping such as salmon, tuna, or egg. The drink is variable and may include green tea, water, or a soft drink. Nutritionally, this bundle is moderate in energy (approximately 540 kcal per set), provides a meaningful source of carbohydrate from the udon and sushi rice, moderate protein from the tofu and sushi, and is relatively low in saturated fat. However, the sodium content is elevated due to the dashi broth and soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the meal is low (approximately 55), driven primarily by the refined wheat udon noodles and sushi rice. This meal set is suitable for a light to moderate lunch or dinner in a Japanese casual dining context and is popular in Singapore's food court and mall restaurant settings.

What is inari tofu in udon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite bundle meal. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for udon noodle soup and inari tofu, blended by estimated component weights. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles as the dominant glycemic component. Confidence is medium because the sushi component is variable (customer choice) and the drink component is excluded from nutrient calculation.
notes: 1. This is a composite bundle meal; the sushi type and drink are variable by customer choice, introducing inherent nutrient variability. The per-100g values represent the estimated solid food components (udon bowl + one average sushi piece) only; the beverage is excluded. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this food type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the available approved source data for this composite meal. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100g, confirmed as negligible/trace in the USDA source data for udon noodle soup and tofu components. 3. The sodium content is high due to dashi broth and soy sauce seasoning; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise caution. 4. The GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific bundle. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; dashi broth typically contains bonito (fish), and sushi toppings may vary. 6. The ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no specific FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for inari udon set meals.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built from component nutrient estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for udon noodle soup and inari tofu components.

Iteration 2: Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' as glycemic_index 55 falls into the low GI category. Updated `gi_source.source_match_notes`, `health_context.type2_diabetes.guidance`, and `llm_training.natural_language_description` to reflect the 'low' GI category. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list; confirmed all Japanese and romanized entries appear verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are documented in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is documented as trace/negligible. Confirmed GI numeric value >0 (55). Confirmed ontology match_type values are all within permitted set. Confirmed g6 multilingual compliance for Japanese cuisine with Japanese script name present.
